digest.editor_note: Fellow Humans, the Middle East just broke the energy markets: oil up 6%, the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, gold through $5,300, and rate-cut bets evaporating in real time. This is not a drill — it is what happens when a supreme leader gets killed and a refinery goes up in smoke simultaneously, and the world discovers, once again, that it has very little in reserve.

sentiment.label: Risk-Off
sentiment.context: US-Israeli strikes on Iran, Strait of Hormuz disruption, oil surging 6%, and collapsing Fed rate-cut expectations are driving a sharp risk-off posture across markets.

stories.headline: Oil Surges 6%, Dollar Rises as Iran War Disrupts Strait of Hormuz
stories.body: Brent crude jumped 6% after US-Israeli strikes killed Iran's Supreme Leader Khamenei and threatened to snarl the Strait of Hormuz, which handles roughly 20% of global oil supply. Ten-year Treasury yields climbed 11 basis points to 4.05% as inflation fears mounted, while traders pushed the first expected Fed rate cut to September — a third cut in 2026 is now nearly fully priced out. Gold topped $5,300 and diesel futures in Europe surged 23%; JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley both called the selloff a buying opportunity in equities, but the bond market is telling a different story.

stories.headline: Ships Come Under Fire Near Strait of Hormuz as Major Carriers Reroute
stories.body: An oil tanker, the Skylight, was attacked near the Strait of Hormuz, with its 20-person crew evacuated by Omani authorities — no party has claimed responsibility. Major shipping companies are now actively avoiding the route, though no official closure has been communicated through recognized maritime channels. The strait carries approximately 20% of the world's daily oil supply, and any sustained disruption compounds the supply shock already priced into energy markets.

stories.headline: Oil Markets Enter Iran Conflict With Minimal Spare Capacity
stories.body: With Brent already climbing on conflict fears, analysts at Barclays flagged that prices could reach $80 a barrel as US-Iran tensions persist — a relatively modest target that underscores how little buffer exists in global supply. The WSJ notes that oil markets have entered this conflict with negligible spare capacity, leaving prices highly sensitive to any further disruption at refineries, pipelines, or export terminals. European natural gas prices soared separately after Qatar shut the world's largest LNG export facility.

stories.headline: India Convenes Emergency Security Council Meeting as West Asia Conflict Escalates
stories.body: Prime Minister Narendra Modi chaired an emergency Cabinet Committee on Security meeting in New Delhi after US-Israeli strikes killed Ayatollah Khamenei, with the session focusing on the safety of roughly nine million Indian nationals living across the Gulf and West Asia. The near-total closure of West Asian airspace has left hundreds of Indians stranded in Dubai, Doha, and other regional transit hubs. Of particular strategic concern: the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for Indian oil imports.

stories.headline: Iran's Soccer Federation Head Doubts Country Will Participate in 2026 World Cup
stories.body: Iranian football federation president Mehdi Taj said Sunday that he cannot envision his country playing World Cup matches on US soil following the American-Israeli bombardment that killed Khamenei. Iran is slated to open Group G play at SoFi Stadium in Los Angeles on June 15 against New Zealand, with further matches scheduled in Seattle. A 40-day national mourning period has already suspended team preparations, and Iranian citizens are subject to a US travel ban — FIFA said it is monitoring the situation but has not commented further.

stories.headline: Amazon India Waives Seller Fees on All Products Under ₹1,000 in Escalating Fee War
stories.body: Amazon India has expanded its zero-commission policy to cover goods priced under ₹1,000, up from ₹300 previously — a move that extends fee-free status to 12.5 crore products and directly targets rivals Meesho and Flipkart. The company reported a 50% surge in new seller sign-ups following its initial fee waiver in April, and estimates the policy will cover 70–80% of its 1.7 million-strong seller base. Amazon declined to disclose the revenue impact of the cuts.

stories.headline: Greg Abel Faces First Real Test as Berkshire Hathaway CEO
stories.body: Warren Buffett's successor Greg Abel is navigating his first major public test at the helm of Berkshire Hathaway, with shareholders watching closely for signals on capital deployment of the company's substantial cash pile. Abel addressed investors directly, rejecting the characterization of Berkshire's cash reserves as a strategic problem — a defense that echoes Buffett's long-standing posture of patient accumulation. How Abel manages the geopolitical shock now hitting energy and equity markets may define his early tenure more than any single acquisition.

quote.text: In the short run, the market is a voting machine, but in the long run, it is a weighing machine.
